OT4ML
Optimal Transport for Machine Learners
Chapters
Optimal Matching between Point CloudsMonge Problem between MeasuresKantorovich RelaxationDual ProblemSemi-discrete and Wasserstein-1Divergences and Dual NormsEntropic Regularization: Sinkhorn AlgorithmEntropic Regularization: ConvergenceGeneralized Wasserstein DistancesGeneralized OT ProblemsBeyond Comparing MeasuresDynamic Optimal TransportWasserstein Gradient FlowsGenerative Models via Transportation
ConclusionNotation TableIndex

Dynamic Optimal Transport

Optimal transport becomes especially powerful once distances between measures are seen as actions of moving mass. This chapter first develops the dynamic language: continuity equations describe admissible measure evolutions, while the Benamou--Brenier formula identifies W2\Wass_2W2​ with a least-action principle. These ideas prepare the gradient-flow and generative-model chapters that follow.

Guiding Comparison

The static formulation optimizes over a coupling between endpoints. The dynamic formulation optimizes over a whole path of measures and a velocity field. The continuity equation is the constraint, and kinetic energy is the cost.

from pathlib import Path
import sys

from IPython.display import Image as DisplayImage
from IPython.display import display

here = Path.cwd()
myst_dir = None
for candidate in [here, here.parent, here / "myst", here.parent / "myst", here.parent.parent / "myst"]:
    if (candidate / "ot4ml_web.py").exists():
        myst_dir = candidate.resolve()
        sys.path.insert(0, str(myst_dir))
        break

if myst_dir is None:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not locate myst/ot4ml_web.py")

repo_root = myst_dir.parent
thumbnails = repo_root / "notebooks-figures" / "thumbnails"

def show_book_figure(name, width=760):
    display(DisplayImage(filename=str(thumbnails / f"{name}.png"), width=width))

Evolutions Over the Space of Measures¶

We start with the continuity equation because it is the common language for particles, densities and weak measure evolutions. It also makes precise which velocity fields actually move mass.

Lagrangian and Eulerian Descriptions¶

Consider an evolution t↦αt∈P(Rd)t\mapsto\alpha_t\in\mathcal P(\RR^d)t↦αt​∈P(Rd). It can be described in a Lagrangian way as the advection of particles along a time-dependent vector field vt(x)v_t(x)vt​(x):

 dx(t) dt=vt(x(t)).\frac{\d x(t)}{\d t}=v_t(x(t)).dtdx(t)​=vt​(x(t)).
(1)

Writing TtT_tTt​ for the associated flow map, so that Tt(x(0))=x(t)T_t(x(0))=x(t)Tt​(x(0))=x(t), the advected measure is

αt=(Tt)♯α0.\alpha_t=(T_t)_\sharp\alpha_0.αt​=(Tt​)♯​α0​.
(2)

For empirical measures, αt=n−1∑i=1nδxi(t)\alpha_t=n^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^n\delta_{x_i(t)}αt​=n−1∑i=1n​δxi​(t)​, each particle solves (1).

In the Eulerian description, the same motion is written directly on the evolving measure:

∂αt∂t+div⁡(vtαt)=0.\frac{\partial\alpha_t}{\partial t} +\operatorname{div}(v_t\alpha_t)=0.∂t∂αt​​+div(vt​αt​)=0.
(3)

This PDE is often called the advection equation, the continuity equation, or Liouville’s equation when it acts on phase space. It is a classical PDE only when αt\alpha_tαt​ has a smooth density. For general measures, and in particular for empirical measures, it is understood weakly: for any smooth test function (t,x)↦φ(t,x)(t,x)\mapsto\varphi(t,x)(t,x)↦φ(t,x) compactly supported in time,

∫01 ⁣∫Rd(∂tφ(t,x)+⟨vt(x),∇xφ(t,x)⟩) dαt(x) dt=0.\int_0^1\!\int_{\RR^d} \left( \partial_t\varphi(t,x) +\dotp{v_t(x)}{\nabla_x\varphi(t,x)} \right) \d\alpha_t(x)\d t =0.∫01​∫Rd​(∂t​φ(t,x)+⟨vt​(x),∇x​φ(t,x)⟩)dαt​(x)dt=0.
(4)

This weak equation is obtained from (3) by integration by parts. For smooth positive densities, the classical and weak formulations are equivalent; for particle clouds, the weak form remains meaningful.

Proposition: Lagrangian Flows Solve the Continuity Equation

Consider a smooth flow Tt:Rd→RdT_t:\RR^d\to\RR^dTt​:Rd→Rd and define αt=(Tt)♯α0\alpha_t=(T_t)_\sharp\alpha_0αt​=(Tt​)♯​α0​. Define the Eulerian velocity field by

vt(Tt(y))=∂tTt(y).v_t(T_t(y))=\partial_t T_t(y).vt​(Tt​(y))=∂t​Tt​(y).
(5)

Then (αt,vt)(\alpha_t,v_t)(αt​,vt​) solves the continuity equation in the weak sense (4). In particular, if α0=n−1∑iδxi(0)\alpha_0=n^{-1}\sum_i\delta_{x_i(0)}α0​=n−1∑i​δxi​(0)​ is empirical, then αt=n−1∑iδxi(t)\alpha_t=n^{-1}\sum_i\delta_{x_i(t)}αt​=n−1∑i​δxi​(t)​ is empirical as well, with particle velocities x˙i(t)=vt(xi(t))\dot x_i(t)=v_t(x_i(t))x˙i​(t)=vt​(xi​(t)).

Proof

Let φ(t,x)\varphi(t,x)φ(t,x) be a smooth test function vanishing at t=0t=0t=0 and t=1t=1t=1. Since αt=(Tt)♯α0\alpha_t=(T_t)_\sharp\alpha_0αt​=(Tt​)♯​α0​,

 d dt∫φ(t,x) dαt(x)= d dt∫φ(t,Tt(y)) dα0(y).\frac{\d}{\d t}\int \varphi(t,x)\d\alpha_t(x) = \frac{\d}{\d t}\int \varphi(t,T_t(y))\d\alpha_0(y).dtd​∫φ(t,x)dαt​(x)=dtd​∫φ(t,Tt​(y))dα0​(y).
(6)

The chain rule gives

 d dt∫φ(t,Tt(y)) dα0(y)=∫(∂tφ(t,Tt(y))+⟨∇xφ(t,Tt(y)),∂tTt(y)⟩) dα0(y).\frac{\d}{\d t}\int \varphi(t,T_t(y))\d\alpha_0(y) = \int \left( \partial_t\varphi(t,T_t(y)) +\dotp{\nabla_x\varphi(t,T_t(y))}{\partial_t T_t(y)} \right) \d\alpha_0(y).dtd​∫φ(t,Tt​(y))dα0​(y)=∫(∂t​φ(t,Tt​(y))+⟨∇x​φ(t,Tt​(y)),∂t​Tt​(y)⟩)dα0​(y).
(7)

Using the definition of vtv_tvt​ and the push-forward relation, this is

∫(∂tφ(t,x)+⟨∇xφ(t,x),vt(x)⟩) dαt(x).\int \left( \partial_t\varphi(t,x)+\dotp{\nabla_x\varphi(t,x)}{v_t(x)} \right) \d\alpha_t(x).∫(∂t​φ(t,x)+⟨∇x​φ(t,x),vt​(x)⟩)dαt​(x).
(8)

Integrating in time and using the boundary values of φ\varphiφ gives (4).

From Measure Evolutions to Vector Fields¶

For a given evolution (αt)t(\alpha_t)_t(αt​)t​, there are typically infinitely many velocity fields vtv_tvt​ satisfying

∂tαt+div⁡(αtvt)=0.\partial_t\alpha_t+\operatorname{div}(\alpha_t v_t)=0.∂t​αt​+div(αt​vt​)=0.
(9)

This non-uniqueness comes from the kernel of the weighted divergence. The linear space of vector fields that leave a measure α\alphaα invariant is

Hα={v:div⁡(αv)=0}.\mathcal H_\alpha = \{v:\operatorname{div}(\alpha v)=0\}.Hα​={v:div(αv)=0}.
(10)

It is usually non-trivial: if α\alphaα is an isotropic Gaussian, Hα\mathcal H_\alphaHα​ contains rotational vector fields generated by anti-symmetric matrices.

Dacorogna--Moser Inversion¶

Reconstructing particles from an observed density evolution is therefore ill-posed. A simple choice, introduced by Dacorogna and Moser Dacorogna & Moser, 1990, imposes that the flux αtvt\alpha_t v_tαt​vt​ is a gradient field. Formally,

vt=−1αt∇Δ−1(∂tαt),v_t = -\frac{1}{\alpha_t} \nabla\Delta^{-1}(\partial_t\alpha_t),vt​=−αt​1​∇Δ−1(∂t​αt​),
(11)

with suitable boundary conditions, for instance vanishing at infinity. This formula is useful conceptually but delicate when αt\alpha_tαt​ vanishes, and it does not generally produce a gradient velocity field.

Least-Square Inversion and Gradient Structure¶

A more robust choice, used implicitly in flow matching, optimal transport and Wasserstein gradient flows, is to select among all admissible velocities the one with smallest kinetic energy:

min⁡v12∫01 ⁣∫Rd∥vt(x)∥2  dαt(x) dtsubject to∂tαt+div⁡(αtvt)=0.\min_v \frac12\int_0^1\!\int_{\RR^d}\norm{v_t(x)}^2\,\d\alpha_t(x)\d t \quad \text{subject to} \quad \partial_t\alpha_t+\operatorname{div}(\alpha_t v_t)=0.vmin​21​∫01​∫Rd​∥vt​(x)∥2dαt​(x)dtsubject to∂t​αt​+div(αt​vt​)=0.
(12)
Proposition: Least-Square Velocities Are Gradients

Assume that αt=ρt  dx\alpha_t=\rho_t\,\d xαt​=ρt​dx is a smooth positive density curve and that boundary terms vanish. The minimizer of (12), if it exists, is a gradient field

vt=∇ϕt,v_t=\nabla\phi_t,vt​=∇ϕt​,
(13)

where ϕt\phi_tϕt​, unique up to an additive constant, solves the weighted Poisson equation

−div⁡(ρt∇ϕt)=∂tρt,vt=−∇Δαt−1(∂tαt),Δαtϕ=div⁡(αt∇ϕ).-\operatorname{div}(\rho_t\nabla\phi_t)=\partial_t\rho_t, \qquad v_t=-\nabla\Delta_{\alpha_t}^{-1}(\partial_t\alpha_t), \qquad \Delta_{\alpha_t}\phi=\operatorname{div}(\alpha_t\nabla\phi).−div(ρt​∇ϕt​)=∂t​ρt​,vt​=−∇Δαt​−1​(∂t​αt​),Δαt​​ϕ=div(αt​∇ϕ).
(14)
Proof

Introduce a Lagrange multiplier ϕt\phi_tϕt​ for the continuity equation. The constrained problem has the formal saddle formulation

min⁡vmax⁡ϕ∫01[12∫Rd∥vt(x)∥2  dαt(x)+∫Rdϕt(x)(div⁡(αtvt)(x)+∂tαt(x)) dx] dt.\min_v\max_\phi \int_0^1 \left[ \frac12\int_{\RR^d}\norm{v_t(x)}^2\,\d\alpha_t(x) + \int_{\RR^d}\phi_t(x) \left(\operatorname{div}(\alpha_t v_t)(x)+\partial_t\alpha_t(x)\right) \d x \right]\d t.vmin​ϕmax​∫01​[21​∫Rd​∥vt​(x)∥2dαt​(x)+∫Rd​ϕt​(x)(div(αt​vt​)(x)+∂t​αt​(x))dx]dt.
(15)

Integrating by parts in the divergence term gives, for each ttt,

∫(12∥vt∥2−⟨∇ϕt,vt⟩) dαt+∫ϕt ∂tαt.\int \left( \frac12\norm{v_t}^2-\dotp{\nabla\phi_t}{v_t} \right) \d\alpha_t + \int\phi_t\,\partial_t\alpha_t.∫(21​∥vt​∥2−⟨∇ϕt​,vt​⟩)dαt​+∫ϕt​∂t​αt​.
(16)

The pointwise minimizer in vtv_tvt​ is therefore vt=∇ϕtv_t=\nabla\phi_tvt​=∇ϕt​. Substituting this into ∂tρt+div⁡(ρtvt)=0\partial_t\rho_t+\operatorname{div}(\rho_t v_t)=0∂t​ρt​+div(ρt​vt​)=0 gives the weighted Poisson equation in (14). The inverse notation is a shorthand for solving this equation on zero-mean right-hand sides, modulo additive constants.

In general this inversion is still computationally demanding, but special choices of (αt)t(\alpha_t)_t(αt​)t​ lead to simpler formulas; this is the mechanism exploited later by flow matching.

Algorithm: Least-square velocity reconstruction

Input: Smooth positive density curve (ρt)t∈[0,1](\rho_t)_{t\in[0,1]}(ρt​)t∈[0,1]​ and boundary conditions.

Output: Minimal-energy velocity field vtv_tvt​ realizing the curve.

For each time ttt do:

Compute ∂tρt\partial_t\rho_t∂t​ρt​.

Solve weighted Poisson equation: −div⁡(ρt∇ϕt)=∂tρt,∫ϕtρt=0.-\diverg(\rho_t\nabla\phi_t)=\partial_t\rho_t, \qquad \int\phi_t\rho_t=0.−div(ρt​∇ϕt​)=∂t​ρt​,∫ϕt​ρt​=0.

Set vt=∇ϕt.v_t=\nabla\phi_t .vt​=∇ϕt​.

Return (vt)t∈[0,1](v_t)_{t\in[0,1]}(vt​)t∈[0,1]​.

Benamou--Brenier Dynamic Formulation of OT¶

The dynamic formulation identifies W2\Wass_2W2​ with the kinetic energy of the cheapest continuity-equation path. It is the point where OT becomes a least-action principle.

Instead of assuming that a whole curve (αt)t∈[0,1](\alpha_t)_{t\in[0,1]}(αt​)t∈[0,1]​ is prescribed, one fixes only the endpoints α0\alpha_0α0​ and α1\alpha_1α1​ and minimizes the least-square energy (12). The theorem of Benamou and Brenier states that this geodesic energy is exactly the squared Wasserstein distance Benamou & Brenier, 2000.

Theorem: Benamou--Brenier

For probability measures α0,α1∈P2(Rd)\alpha_0,\alpha_1\in\mathcal P_2(\RR^d)α0​,α1​∈P2​(Rd),

W22(α0,α1)=inf⁡(αt,vt)∫01 ⁣∫Rd∥vt(x)∥2  dαt(x) dt,\Wass_2^2(\alpha_0,\alpha_1) = \inf_{(\alpha_t,v_t)} \int_0^1\!\int_{\RR^d}\norm{v_t(x)}^2\,\d\alpha_t(x)\d t,W22​(α0​,α1​)=(αt​,vt​)inf​∫01​∫Rd​∥vt​(x)∥2dαt​(x)dt,
(17)

where the infimum is over (αt,vt)(\alpha_t,v_t)(αt​,vt​) solving ∂tαt+∇ ⁣⋅(αtvt)=0\partial_t\alpha_t+\nabla\!\cdot(\alpha_t v_t)=0∂t​αt​+∇⋅(αt​vt​)=0 with αt=0=α0\alpha_{t=0}=\alpha_0αt=0​=α0​ and αt=1=α1\alpha_{t=1}=\alpha_1αt=1​=α1​. If α0\alpha_0α0​ has a density and TTT is the optimal Monge map T♯α0=α1T_\sharp\alpha_0=\alpha_1T♯​α0​=α1​, the minimizer is

αt=((1−t)Id+tT)♯α0,vt((1−t)x+tT(x))=T(x)−x.\alpha_t=((1-t)\Id+tT)_\sharp\alpha_0, \qquad v_t((1-t)x+tT(x))=T(x)-x.αt​=((1−t)Id+tT)♯​α0​,vt​((1−t)x+tT(x))=T(x)−x.
(18)
Proof

For the inequality “dynamic ≤\leq≤ static”, assume first that a Monge map TTT exists and define (αt,vt)(\alpha_t,v_t)(αt​,vt​) by (18). Since the Lagrangian velocity T(x)−xT(x)-xT(x)−x is independent of ttt,

∫01 ⁣∫∥vt∥2  dαt dt=∫∥T(x)−x∥2  dα0(x),\int_0^1\!\int\norm{v_t}^2\,\d\alpha_t\d t = \int\norm{T(x)-x}^2\,\d\alpha_0(x),∫01​∫∥vt​∥2dαt​dt=∫∥T(x)−x∥2dα0​(x),
(19)

so the dynamic cost is no larger than the static Monge cost. Without a Monge map, the same construction uses an optimal coupling π\piπ: sample (X,Y)∼π(X,Y)\sim\pi(X,Y)∼π and move along the straight path γX,Y(t)=(1−t)X+tY\gamma_{X,Y}(t)=(1-t)X+tYγX,Y​(t)=(1−t)X+tY. This path measure has action ∫∥x−y∥2 dπ(x,y)\int\norm{x-y}^2\d\pi(x,y)∫∥x−y∥2dπ(x,y); projecting path velocities onto their conditional mean at time ttt gives an admissible Eulerian velocity with no larger action, so the dynamic value is no larger than the Kantorovich value.

Conversely, for a smooth deterministic path, take the flow TtT_tTt​ defined by T˙t=vt∘Tt\dot T_t=v_t\circ T_tT˙t​=vt​∘Tt​ and T0=IdT_0=\IdT0​=Id. Then αt=(Tt)♯α0\alpha_t=(T_t)_\sharp\alpha_0αt​=(Tt​)♯​α0​ and (T1)♯α0=α1(T_1)_\sharp\alpha_0=\alpha_1(T1​)♯​α0​=α1​. Jensen’s inequality gives

∥T1(x)−x∥2≤∫01∥vt(Tt(x))∥2 dt.\norm{T_1(x)-x}^2 \leq \int_0^1\norm{v_t(T_t(x))}^2\d t.∥T1​(x)−x∥2≤∫01​∥vt​(Tt​(x))∥2dt.
(20)

After integration with respect to α0\alpha_0α0​, the Monge cost is bounded above by the dynamic action. For general finite-energy solutions of the continuity equation, the superposition principle lifts the curve to a probability measure on absolutely continuous paths; applying Jensen’s inequality pathwise gives a coupling of the endpoints whose quadratic cost is no larger than the action. Thus the Kantorovich value is bounded above by the dynamic value.

Although (17) is not jointly convex in (αt,vt)(\alpha_t,v_t)(αt​,vt​), it becomes convex after replacing velocities by the momentum measure mt=vtαtm_t=v_t\alpha_tmt​=vt​αt​ and using the perspective action. In the absolutely continuous case αt=ρt  dx\alpha_t=\rho_t\,\d xαt​=ρt​dx and mt(x)=ρt(x)vt(x)m_t(x)=\rho_t(x)v_t(x)mt​(x)=ρt​(x)vt​(x),

W22(α0,α1)=inf⁡∂tρt+div⁡mt=0ρt=0dx=α0, ρt=1dx=α1∫01 ⁣∫Rd∥mt(x)∥2ρt(x) dx  dt,\Wass_2^2(\alpha_0,\alpha_1) = \inf_{\substack{\partial_t\rho_t+\operatorname{div}m_t=0\\ \rho_{t=0}\d x=\alpha_0,\ \rho_{t=1}\d x=\alpha_1}} \int_0^1\!\int_{\RR^d} \frac{\norm{m_t(x)}^2}{\rho_t(x)} \d x\,\d t,W22​(α0​,α1​)=∂t​ρt​+divmt​=0ρt=0​dx=α0​, ρt=1​dx=α1​​inf​∫01​∫Rd​ρt​(x)∥mt​(x)∥2​dxdt,
(21)

with the usual convention that the integrand is 0 when (ρt,mt)=(0,0)(\rho_t,m_t)=(0,0)(ρt​,mt​)=(0,0) and +∞+\infty+∞ when ρt=0\rho_t=0ρt​=0 but mt≠0m_t\neq0mt​=0. For singular endpoints or curves, the same statement is interpreted with vector-valued momentum measures and the corresponding recession convention. This convex reformulation enables geodesic interpolation by convex optimization once the domain is discretized.

The momentum formulation also has a useful dual. It turns the least-action problem into a Hamilton--Jacobi subsolution inequality for a scalar potential, with equality on the part of space-time actually visited by the optimal curve. With the no-1/21/21/2 convention of (21), the constants are as follows.

Proposition: Dual Benamou--Brenier Problem

Assume, for simplicity, that the densities are smooth, compactly supported, and that boundary terms vanish. Then the convex dynamic value has the dual formulation

W22(α0,α1)=sup⁡ϕ{∫Rdϕ1  dα1−∫Rdϕ0  dα0  :  ∂tϕt+14∥∇ϕt∥2≤0}.\Wass_2^2(\alpha_0,\alpha_1) = \sup_{\phi} \left\{ \int_{\RR^d}\phi_1\,\d\alpha_1 - \int_{\RR^d}\phi_0\,\d\alpha_0 \;:\; \partial_t\phi_t+\frac14\norm{\nabla\phi_t}^2\leq 0 \right\}.W22​(α0​,α1​)=ϕsup​{∫Rd​ϕ1​dα1​−∫Rd​ϕ0​dα0​:∂t​ϕt​+41​∥∇ϕt​∥2≤0}.
(22)

If (ρ,m)(\rho,m)(ρ,m) and ϕ\phiϕ are smooth primal and dual optimizers, then

mt=ρt2∇ϕt,∂tϕt+14∥∇ϕt∥2=0on {ρt>0}.m_t=\frac{\rho_t}{2}\nabla\phi_t, \qquad \partial_t\phi_t+\frac14\norm{\nabla\phi_t}^2=0 \quad\text{on }\{\rho_t>0\}.mt​=2ρt​​∇ϕt​,∂t​ϕt​+41​∥∇ϕt​∥2=0on {ρt​>0}.
(23)

Equivalently, the optimal Eulerian velocity is vt=mt/ρt=∇ϕt/2v_t=m_t/\rho_t=\nabla\phi_t/2vt​=mt​/ρt​=∇ϕt​/2.

Proof

Let (ρ,m)(\rho,m)(ρ,m) satisfy the continuity equation and let ϕ\phiϕ be smooth. Multiplying the constraint by ϕ\phiϕ and integrating by parts gives

∫Rdϕ1  dα1−∫Rdϕ0  dα0=∫01 ⁣∫Rd(ρt ∂tϕt+⟨mt,∇ϕt⟩) dx dt.\int_{\RR^d}\phi_1\,\d\alpha_1 - \int_{\RR^d}\phi_0\,\d\alpha_0 = \int_0^1\!\int_{\RR^d} \left(\rho_t\,\partial_t\phi_t+\dotp{m_t}{\nabla\phi_t}\right)\d x\d t .∫Rd​ϕ1​dα1​−∫Rd​ϕ0​dα0​=∫01​∫Rd​(ρt​∂t​ϕt​+⟨mt​,∇ϕt​⟩)dxdt.
(24)

If ∂tϕt+∥∇ϕt∥2/4≤0\partial_t\phi_t+\norm{\nabla\phi_t}^2/4\leq0∂t​ϕt​+∥∇ϕt​∥2/4≤0, Young’s inequality yields

ρ ∂tϕ+⟨m,∇ϕ⟩≤−ρ4∥∇ϕ∥2+⟨m,∇ϕ⟩≤∥m∥2ρ,\rho\,\partial_t\phi+\dotp{m}{\nabla\phi} \leq -\frac{\rho}{4}\norm{\nabla\phi}^2+\dotp{m}{\nabla\phi} \leq \frac{\norm m^2}{\rho},ρ∂t​ϕ+⟨m,∇ϕ⟩≤−4ρ​∥∇ϕ∥2+⟨m,∇ϕ⟩≤ρ∥m∥2​,
(25)

with the usual perspective convention. Thus the dual objective of every feasible potential is no larger than the action of every feasible primal pair. Conversely, introducing ϕ\phiϕ as a Lagrange multiplier for ∂tρ+div⁡m=0\partial_t\rho+\operatorname{div}m=0∂t​ρ+divm=0, and discarding the fixed endpoint contribution, the pointwise minimization contains ∥m∥2/ρ−ρ ∂tϕ−⟨m,∇ϕ⟩\norm m^2/\rho-\rho\,\partial_t\phi-\dotp{m}{\nabla\phi}∥m∥2/ρ−ρ∂t​ϕ−⟨m,∇ϕ⟩. Minimizing over mmm gives m=ρ∇ϕ/2m=\rho\nabla\phi/2m=ρ∇ϕ/2; minimizing over ρ≥0\rho\geq0ρ≥0 is finite exactly under ∂tϕ+∥∇ϕ∥2/4≤0\partial_t\phi+\norm{\nabla\phi}^2/4\leq0∂t​ϕ+∥∇ϕ∥2/4≤0. Fenchel--Rockafellar duality then gives no duality gap in finite-dimensional discretizations. The continuum identity follows by the usual relaxation and approximation, with ϕ\phiϕ interpreted as a Hamilton--Jacobi subsolution. Equality in the two pointwise inequalities gives (23).

The inequality in (22) is the subsolution form of the Hamilton--Jacobi equation associated with the Hamiltonian H(p)=∥p∥2/4H(p)=\norm{p}^2/4H(p)=∥p∥2/4. On the transported mass, equality holds and the characteristics satisfy x˙=∇pH(∇ϕ)=∇ϕ/2\dot x=\nabla_pH(\nabla\phi)=\nabla\phi/2x˙=∇p​H(∇ϕ)=∇ϕ/2. Since this Hamiltonian is independent of xxx in Euclidean space, the momentum along characteristics is constant and the trajectories are straight lines. On a Riemannian manifold, the same duality uses the Hamiltonian Hx(p)=∥p∥gx−12/4H_x(p)=\norm{p}_{g_x^{-1}}^2/4Hx​(p)=∥p∥gx−1​2​/4; the characteristics are then geodesics for the underlying metric.

This also recovers the static Kantorovich inequality from a dynamic principle. If γ\gammaγ is any smooth curve with γ(0)=x\gamma(0)=xγ(0)=x and γ(1)=y\gamma(1)=yγ(1)=y, then

 d dtϕt(γ(t))=∂tϕt(γ(t))+⟨∇ϕt(γ(t)),γ˙(t)⟩≤∥γ˙(t)∥2.\frac{\d}{\d t}\phi_t(\gamma(t)) = \partial_t\phi_t(\gamma(t))+\dotp{\nabla\phi_t(\gamma(t))}{\dot\gamma(t)} \leq \norm{\dot\gamma(t)}^2.dtd​ϕt​(γ(t))=∂t​ϕt​(γ(t))+⟨∇ϕt​(γ(t)),γ˙​(t)⟩≤∥γ˙​(t)∥2.
(26)

After integration and minimization over curves,

ϕ1(y)−ϕ0(x)≤∥x−y∥2.\phi_1(y)-\phi_0(x)\leq \norm{x-y}^2.ϕ1​(y)−ϕ0​(x)≤∥x−y∥2.
(27)

Thus (ϕ0,ϕ1)(\phi_0,\phi_1)(ϕ0​,ϕ1​) is a feasible static Kantorovich dual pair for the quadratic cost. At optimality the inequality is saturated on the endpoint pairs connected by the primal characteristics.

show_book_figure("dynamic-benamou-brenier-duality")

One-dimensional Benamou--Brenier primal and dual solutions. The endpoints are Gaussian mixtures and the solution is computed from monotone quantile interpolation. The panels show the primal density, the momentum mt=ρtvtm_t=\rho_t v_tmt​=ρt​vt​, and the dual Hamilton--Jacobi potential. Along the active transported mass, the notebook checks mt=ρt∂xϕt/2m_t=\rho_t\partial_x\phi_t/2mt​=ρt​∂x​ϕt​/2 and ∂tϕt+∣∂xϕt∣2/4=0\partial_t\phi_t+|\partial_x\phi_t|^2/4=0∂t​ϕt​+∣∂x​ϕt​∣2/4=0.

show_book_figure("dynamic-benamou-brenier-geodesic")

Benamou--Brenier geodesic between two sampled silhouettes. A discrete quadratic OT plan between finely subsampled cat and two-disks point clouds induces the McCann interpolation Zt=(1−t)X+tYZ_t=(1-t)X+tYZt​=(1−t)X+tY, which is the Lagrangian realization of the least-action solution. The left panel renders local color images of the smaller-bandwidth kernel-smoothed densities with enough padding to include the full silhouettes. The right panel overlays shortened velocity arrows centered at evenly subsampled midpoint particles Z1/2Z_{1/2}Z1/2​; each displayed arrow runs in data coordinates from a source-side tail to a target-side head along the matched characteristic direction Y−XY-XY−X, but is not drawn as the full endpoint segment from XXX to YYY.

The interactive demo keeps the same Lagrangian picture: particles are matched once, then move along straight characteristics. The time and velocity scale controls separate the path αt\alpha_tαt​ from the underlying displacement field.

Interactive panel. Use the time and velocity-scale controls to follow the Benamou-Brenier geodesic as a moving density with an Eulerian velocity field.

Path-Space Formulation¶

Let S=C([0,1];Rd)\Ss=C([0,1];\RR^d)S=C([0,1];Rd) be the space of continuous paths endowed with the uniform topology. For t∈[0,1]t\in[0,1]t∈[0,1] define the evaluation map

Pt:S→Rd,Pt(γ)=γ(t).P_t:\Ss\to\RR^d, \qquad P_t(\gamma)=\gamma(t).Pt​:S→Rd,Pt​(γ)=γ(t).
(28)

The Benamou--Brenier cost admits the equivalent formulation

W22(α0,α1)=inf⁡M∈P(S){∫S ⁣∫01∥γ˙(t)∥2 dt  dM(γ)  :  (P0)♯M=α0, (P1)♯M=α1}.\Wass_2^2(\alpha_0,\alpha_1) = \inf_{M\in\Pp(\Ss)} \enscond{ \int_{\Ss}\!\int_0^1\norm{\dot\gamma(t)}^2\d t\,\d M(\gamma) }{ (P_0)_\sharp M=\alpha_0,\ (P_1)_\sharp M=\alpha_1 }.W22​(α0​,α1​)=M∈P(S)inf​{∫S​∫01​∥γ˙​(t)∥2dtdM(γ):(P0​)♯​M=α0​, (P1​)♯​M=α1​}.
(29)

If α0\alpha_0α0​ has a density, the minimizer M∗M^*M∗ is unique. Its time marginals reproduce the optimal curve: αt=(Pt)♯M∗\alpha_t=(P_t)_\sharp M^*αt​=(Pt​)♯​M∗ for all ttt. Furthermore, for a.e. ttt, denoting Qt(γ)=γ˙(t)Q_t(\gamma)=\dot\gamma(t)Qt​(γ)=γ˙​(t) on absolutely continuous paths, the conditional law of the velocity is deterministic:

(Pt,Qt)♯M∗( dx, dq)=αt( dx)δvt∗(x)( dq),(P_t,Q_t)_\sharp M^*(\d x,\d q) = \alpha_t(\d x)\delta_{v_t^*(x)}(\d q),(Pt​,Qt​)♯​M∗(dx,dq)=αt​(dx)δvt∗​(x)​(dq),
(30)

where vt∗v_t^*vt∗​ is the optimal velocity field in the Benamou--Brenier formulation. Hence M∗M^*M∗ concentrates on straight-line geodesics and, for a.e. ttt, assigns exactly one direction at αt\alpha_tαt​-a.e. spatial point.

Extensions of the Dynamic Formulation¶

The same variational grammar extends beyond the quadratic Wasserstein distance. One changes either the kinetic exponent, the mobility or the balance equation, while keeping a continuity-type constraint and a convex perspective action.

Generalized Benamou--Brenier Distances¶

The dynamic formulation is not specific to W2\Wass_2W2​. For measures with finite ppp-th moments and p>1p>1p>1, one has the analogous action formula

Wpp(α0,α1)=inf⁡∂tαt+∇⋅(αtvt)=0αt=0=α0, αt=1=α1∫01 ⁣∫Rd∣vt(x)∣p  dαt(x)  dt.\Wass_p^p(\alpha_0,\alpha_1) = \inf_{\substack{\partial_t\alpha_t+\nabla\!\cdot(\alpha_t v_t)=0\\ \alpha_{t=0}=\alpha_0,\ \alpha_{t=1}=\alpha_1}} \int_0^1\!\int_{\RR^d} |v_t(x)|^p\,\d\alpha_t(x)\,\d t.Wpp​(α0​,α1​)=∂t​αt​+∇⋅(αt​vt​)=0αt=0​=α0​, αt=1​=α1​​inf​∫01​∫Rd​∣vt​(x)∣pdαt​(x)dt.
(31)

When αt=ρt  dx\alpha_t=\rho_t\,\d xαt​=ρt​dx and mt=ρtvtm_t=\rho_t v_tmt​=ρt​vt​, this becomes the convex perspective action

∫01 ⁣∫Rd∣mt(x)∣pρt(x)p−1  dx  dt,∂tρt+∇ ⁣⋅mt=0,\int_0^1\!\int_{\RR^d} \frac{|m_t(x)|^p}{\rho_t(x)^{p-1}}\,\d x\,\d t, \qquad \partial_t\rho_t+\nabla\!\cdot m_t=0,∫01​∫Rd​ρt​(x)p−1∣mt​(x)∣p​dxdt,∂t​ρt​+∇⋅mt​=0,
(32)

with the usual convention that the integrand is 0 if (ρ,m)=(0,0)(\rho,m)=(0,0)(ρ,m)=(0,0) and +∞+\infty+∞ if ρ=0\rho=0ρ=0 but m≠0m\neq0m=0.

A second class of variants changes the mobility of the medium: the quadratic action ∣m∣2/ρ|m|^2/\rho∣m∣2/ρ is replaced by ∣m∣2/θ(ρ)|m|^2/\theta(\rho)∣m∣2/θ(ρ) for a suitable concave mobility θ\thetaθ. Under appropriate structural assumptions, this produces transport metrics adapted to nonlinear diffusions and finite-volume discretizations Dolbeault et al., 2009. The finite-state version, where the mobility is dictated by a reversible Markov chain, is developed in Discrete Wasserstein Geometries on Markov Chains. These extensions keep the same variational grammar as Benamou--Brenier: a continuity-type constraint, an action density, and geodesics obtained by minimizing an integrated kinetic cost.

Dynamic Unbalanced OT¶

Unbalanced dynamic transport is obtained by allowing mass to be created and destroyed along the path. The continuity equation is replaced by a balance equation, and the action penalizes both spatial motion and growth. This dynamic formulation underlies the Hellinger--Kantorovich and Wasserstein--Fisher--Rao metrics Liero et al., 2016Chizat et al., 2018; its equivalence with static entropy-transport and cone formulations is developed in Liero et al., 2018Chizat et al., 2018.

A representative quadratic action is

∂tρt+∇ ⁣⋅mt=st,∫01 ⁣∫(∣mt∣2ρt+κ2st2ρt) dx  dt,\partial_t\rho_t+\nabla\!\cdot m_t=s_t, \qquad \int_0^1\!\int \left( \frac{|m_t|^2}{\rho_t} +\kappa^2\frac{s_t^2}{\rho_t} \right)\d x\,\d t,∂t​ρt​+∇⋅mt​=st​,∫01​∫(ρt​∣mt​∣2​+κ2ρt​st2​​)dxdt,
(33)

with the same perspective convention as above. Equivalently, writing mt=ρtvtm_t=\rho_t v_tmt​=ρt​vt​ and st=ρtgts_t=\rho_t g_tst​=ρt​gt​, one minimizes ∫01∫(∣vt∣2+κ2gt2) dρt dt\int_0^1\int(|v_t|^2+\kappa^2g_t^2)\d\rho_t\d t∫01​∫(∣vt​∣2+κ2gt2​)dρt​dt under

∂tρt+∇ ⁣⋅(ρtvt)=gtρt.\partial_t\rho_t+\nabla\!\cdot(\rho_t v_t)=g_t\rho_t.∂t​ρt​+∇⋅(ρt​vt​)=gt​ρt​.
(34)

The parameter κ\kappaκ fixes the relative cost of reaction and transport: changing it rescales the radial/angular balance in the associated cone metric. For measure-valued triples, the action is understood in the lower-semicontinuous perspective sense

Aκ(ρ,m,s):=∫(∥m˙∥2ρ˙+κ2s˙2ρ˙) dλ,(ρ˙,m˙,s˙)=( dρ dλ, dm dλ, ds dλ),\mathcal A_\kappa(\rho,m,s) \eqdef \int \left( \frac{\norm{\dot m}^2}{\dot\rho} + \kappa^2\frac{\dot s^2}{\dot\rho} \right)\d\lambda, \qquad (\dot\rho,\dot m,\dot s) = \left( \frac{\d\rho}{\d\lambda}, \frac{\d m}{\d\lambda}, \frac{\d s}{\d\lambda} \right),Aκ​(ρ,m,s):=∫(ρ˙​∥m˙∥2​+κ2ρ˙​s˙2​)dλ,(ρ˙​,m˙,s˙)=(dλdρ​,dλdm​,dλds​),
(35)

where λ\lambdaλ dominates ρ\rhoρ and the total variations of mmm and sss. The value is independent of this choice. The convention is 0/0=00/0=00/0=0 and a/0=+∞a/0=+\inftya/0=+∞ for a>0a>0a>0, so finite action forces both the flux and the source to be absolutely continuous with respect to the transported mass.

Proposition: Static/Dynamic Equivalence for Unbalanced OT

Fix the action above and let CWκ\mathcal C\mathcal W_\kappaCWκ​ be the cone value of the cone formulation of unbalanced OT, with the cone metric normalized to the same growth scale κ\kappaκ. For nonnegative finite measures α0,α1\alpha_0,\alpha_1α0​,α1​ on Rd\RR^dRd, the dynamic value

WFRκ2(α0,α1):=inf⁡∂tρt+∇⋅mt=stρ0=α0, ρ1=α1∫01Aκ(ρt,mt,st)  dt\mathrm{WFR}_\kappa^2(\alpha_0,\alpha_1) \eqdef \inf_{\substack{\partial_t\rho_t+\nabla\cdot m_t=s_t\\ \rho_0=\alpha_0,\ \rho_1=\alpha_1}} \int_0^1 \mathcal A_\kappa(\rho_t,m_t,s_t)\,\d tWFRκ2​(α0​,α1​):=∂t​ρt​+∇⋅mt​=st​ρ0​=α0​, ρ1​=α1​​inf​∫01​Aκ​(ρt​,mt​,st​)dt
(36)

equals the static cone formulation CWκ(α0,α1)\mathcal C\mathcal W_\kappa(\alpha_0,\alpha_1)CWκ​(α0​,α1​). Hence the static unbalanced problem and the balance-equation least-action problem define the same geodesic distance.

Proof

The cone construction turns variation of mass into radial motion and spatial transport into angular motion on C[Rd]\mathfrak C[\RR^d]C[Rd]. Applying the Benamou--Brenier theorem on the cone to the lifted endpoint measures gives a dynamic least-action problem on C[Rd]\mathfrak C[\RR^d]C[Rd] whose static value is the cone value. This is the standard static/dynamic identification for the Hellinger--Kantorovich and Wasserstein--Fisher--Rao metrics Liero et al., 2016Liero et al., 2018Chizat et al., 2018Chizat et al., 2018.

Projecting a cone curve back to the base space with weight r2r^2r2 produces a measure curve ρt\rho_tρt​, a spatial flux mtm_tmt​ and a source term sts_tst​ satisfying the balance equation. With the matching normalization of the cone metric, the cone kinetic energy decomposes exactly into the perspective action Aκ\mathcal A_\kappaAκ​ in (36). Conversely, any finite-action triple (ρt,mt,st)(\rho_t,m_t,s_t)(ρt​,mt​,st​) can be lifted to a cone curve whose radial velocity realizes the growth term and whose spatial velocity realizes the transport term, with the same action after relaxation. The two infima are therefore equal; lower semicontinuity gives the general finite-measure statement from the smooth positive case.

show_book_figure("dynamic-unbalanced-geodesic")

Balanced and unbalanced Sinkhorn-barycenter interpolations between two one-dimensional Gaussian mixtures with swapped modal masses. The balanced row conserves total mass, so excess mass from the dominant left mode must move along the line toward the dominant right target mode, producing transient mass in the middle. The unbalanced row uses KL-relaxed marginal constraints; mass can be attenuated near overrepresented modes and recreated near underrepresented modes, giving a reaction--transport interpolation closer to the Wasserstein--Fisher--Rao intuition.

The interactive demo below exposes this balance directly. A high reaction weight keeps more mass local by fading and recreating modes, while the balanced path must carry mass through space.

Interactive panel. Use the growth and time controls to compare motion with source terms in dynamic unbalanced transport.

Algorithm: Douglas--Rachford for dynamic Benamou--Brenier

Input: Functionals F,G=ιC\mathcal F,\mathcal G=\iota_{\mathcal C}F,G=ιC​, proximal parameter τ>0\tau>0τ>0, initial field Z0Z^0Z0.

Output: Discrete density-momentum field U⋆U^\starU⋆.

For k=0,1,…k=0,1,\ldotsk=0,1,… do:

Uk+1=prox⁡τF(Zk).U^{k+1}=\prox_{\tau\mathcal F}(Z^k).Uk+1=proxτF​(Zk).

Project reflected point: U~k+1=prox⁡τG(2Uk+1−Zk)=Proj⁡C(2Uk+1−Zk).\widetilde U^{k+1} = \prox_{\tau\mathcal G}(2U^{k+1}-Z^k) = \Proj_{\mathcal C}(2U^{k+1}-Z^k).Uk+1=proxτG​(2Uk+1−Zk)=ProjC​(2Uk+1−Zk).

Update Zk+1=Zk+U~k+1−Uk+1.Z^{k+1}=Z^k+\widetilde U^{k+1}-U^{k+1}.Zk+1=Zk+Uk+1−Uk+1.

If ∥Uk+1−U~k+1∥≤tol\norm{U^{k+1}-\widetilde U^{k+1}}\leq\mathrm{tol}∥∥​Uk+1−Uk+1∥∥​≤tol then:

Return Uk+1U^{k+1}Uk+1.

Discrete Wasserstein Geometries on Markov Chains¶

The Benamou--Brenier construction also has a finite-state analogue, but not the naive one obtained by putting the Euclidean metric on the simplex. The key idea, introduced by Maas and independently developed in related forms by Mielke and by Chow--Huang--Li--Zhou, is to use the transition graph of a reversible Markov chain to define the admissible directions and the mobility of the mass Maas, 2011Mielke, 2013Chow et al., 2012. The resulting distance turns the heat flow of the chain into the gradient flow of the discrete Shannon entropy. This is the finite-dimensional counterpart of the fact that the heat equation on Rd\RR^dRd is the Wasserstein gradient flow of entropy, and it is also the geometric structure used in numerical work on discrete metric-measure spaces Erbar & Maas, 2014Erbar et al., 2017.

Let X={1,…,n}\mathcal X=\{1,\ldots,n\}X={1,…,n} and let K=(Kij)K=(K_{ij})K=(Kij​) denote the off-diagonal transition rates of an irreducible continuous-time Markov chain which is reversible with respect to a probability vector π\piπ, so that πiKij=πjKji\pi_iK_{ij}=\pi_jK_{ji}πi​Kij​=πj​Kji​ for i≠ji\neq ji=j. We write a probability p∈Σnp\in\Sigma_np∈Σn​ in density form pi=πiρip_i=\pi_i\rho_ipi​=πi​ρi​, where

Σn:={p∈R+n:∑ipi=1}.\Sigma_n\eqdef\left\{p\in\RR_+^n:\sum_i p_i=1\right\}.Σn​:={p∈R+n​:i∑​pi​=1}.
(37)

The entropy relative to π\piπ is

Ent⁡π(ρ):=∑iπiρilog⁡ρi.\operatorname{Ent}_\pi(\rho)\eqdef\sum_i\pi_i\rho_i\log\rho_i.Entπ​(ρ):=i∑​πi​ρi​logρi​.
(38)

The logarithmic mean

θ(a,b):={a−blog⁡a−log⁡b,a≠b,a,a=b,\theta(a,b)\eqdef \begin{cases} \displaystyle\frac{a-b}{\log a-\log b}, & a\neq b,\\[.4em] a, & a=b, \end{cases}θ(a,b):=⎩⎨⎧​loga−logba−b​,a,​a=b,a=b,​
(39)

is the specific mobility which makes the entropy gradient exactly coincide with the Markov evolution. For a potential ψ∈Rn\psi\in\RR^nψ∈Rn, define the Onsager operator

(Kρψ)i:=∑jKijθ(ρi,ρj)(ψi−ψj).(\mathcal K_\rho\psi)_i \eqdef \sum_j K_{ij}\theta(\rho_i,\rho_j)(\psi_i-\psi_j).(Kρ​ψ)i​:=j∑​Kij​θ(ρi​,ρj​)(ψi​−ψj​).
(40)

Its associated action is

A(ρ,ψ):=12∑i,jπiKijθ(ρi,ρj)(ψi−ψj)2.\mathcal A(\rho,\psi) \eqdef \frac12\sum_{i,j}\pi_iK_{ij}\theta(\rho_i,\rho_j)(\psi_i-\psi_j)^2.A(ρ,ψ):=21​i,j∑​πi​Kij​θ(ρi​,ρj​)(ψi​−ψj​)2.
(41)

The discrete transport distance is the least action

WK2(ρ0,ρ1):=inf⁡ρt,ψt∫01A(ρt,ψt)  dt,ρ˙t+Kρtψt=0,\mathcal W_K^2(\rho^0,\rho^1) \eqdef \inf_{\rho_t,\psi_t} \int_0^1\mathcal A(\rho_t,\psi_t)\,\d t, \qquad \dot\rho_t+\mathcal K_{\rho_t}\psi_t=0,WK2​(ρ0,ρ1):=ρt​,ψt​inf​∫01​A(ρt​,ψt​)dt,ρ˙​t​+Kρt​​ψt​=0,
(42)

with endpoints ρ0=ρ0\rho_0=\rho^0ρ0​=ρ0, ρ1=ρ1\rho_1=\rho^1ρ1​=ρ1. Equivalently, one can write the same formula in edge-flux variables, exactly as in a finite-volume discretization: the flux is only allowed along edges where Kij>0K_{ij}>0Kij​>0, and the denominator in the kinetic energy is the logarithmic mean of the two endpoint densities.

Proposition: Entropy Gradient Flow of a Reversible Markov Chain

Let KKK be reversible with invariant law π\piπ. The gradient flow of Ent⁡π\operatorname{Ent}_\piEntπ​ for the metric WK\mathcal W_KWK​ is the forward equation of the Markov chain,

ρ˙i(t)=∑jKij(ρj(t)−ρi(t)).\dot\rho_i(t)=\sum_jK_{ij}\bigl(\rho_j(t)-\rho_i(t)\bigr).ρ˙​i​(t)=j∑​Kij​(ρj​(t)−ρi​(t)).
(43)

Equivalently, for the masses pi(t)=πiρi(t)p_i(t)=\pi_i\rho_i(t)pi​(t)=πi​ρi​(t), this is p˙i(t)=∑j(pj(t)Kji−pi(t)Kij)\dot p_i(t)=\sum_j(p_j(t)K_{ji}-p_i(t)K_{ij})p˙​i​(t)=∑j​(pj​(t)Kji​−pi​(t)Kij​).

Proof

The first variation of the entropy, with respect to the weighted pairing ∑iπiξiφi\sum_i\pi_i\xi_i\varphi_i∑i​πi​ξi​φi​, is log⁡ρi+1\log\rho_i+1logρi​+1. Constants do not contribute to Kρ\mathcal K_\rhoKρ​, hence the metric gradient-flow equation is

ρ˙=−Kρlog⁡ρ.\dot\rho=-\mathcal K_\rho\log\rho.ρ˙​=−Kρ​logρ.
(44)

Using the identity

θ(a,b)(log⁡a−log⁡b)=a−b,\theta(a,b)(\log a-\log b)=a-b,θ(a,b)(loga−logb)=a−b,
(45)

one obtains, componentwise,

ρ˙i=−∑jKijθ(ρi,ρj)(log⁡ρi−log⁡ρj)=∑jKij(ρj−ρi),\dot\rho_i =-\sum_jK_{ij}\theta(\rho_i,\rho_j)(\log\rho_i-\log\rho_j) = \sum_jK_{ij}(\rho_j-\rho_i),ρ˙​i​=−j∑​Kij​θ(ρi​,ρj​)(logρi​−logρj​)=j∑​Kij​(ρj​−ρi​),
(46)

which is the density form of the Kolmogorov forward equation. Multiplying by πi\pi_iπi​ and using detailed balance gives the equation for the probability masses.

Although the minimizing-movement construction is introduced only in Paragraph, it is useful to keep its intuition in mind here. If one formally performs one JKO step for the metric WK\mathcal W_KWK​,

ρk+1∈arg min⁡ρ12τWK2(ρ,ρk)+Ent⁡π(ρ),\rho^{k+1}\in\argmin_\rho \frac{1}{2\tau}\mathcal W_K^2(\rho,\rho^k)+\operatorname{Ent}_\pi(\rho),ρk+1∈ρargmin​2τ1​WK2​(ρ,ρk)+Entπ​(ρ),
(47)

then the first-order optimality condition gives, for small τ\tauτ,

ρk+1−ρkτ=−Kρklog⁡ρk+O(τ)=Kρk+O(τ),\frac{\rho^{k+1}-\rho^k}{\tau} =-\mathcal K_{\rho^k}\log\rho^k+O(\tau) =K\rho^k+O(\tau),τρk+1−ρk​=−Kρk​logρk+O(τ)=Kρk+O(τ),
(48)

where (Kρ)i=∑jKij(ρj−ρi)(K\rho)_i=\sum_jK_{ij}(\rho_j-\rho_i)(Kρ)i​=∑j​Kij​(ρj​−ρi​). Thus the discrete Wasserstein geometry is engineered so that the implicit Euler step for entropy is, to first order, the Markov semigroup. This is precisely the finite-state analogue of the JKO interpretation of the heat equation.

Closed Forms in Dimensions Two and Three¶

For the two- and three-state examples below, take the uniform random walk on the complete neighbor graph, i.e. every pair of distinct states is declared neighboring. Thus πi=1/n\pi_i=1/nπi​=1/n, and Kij=1/(n−1)K_{ij}=1/(n-1)Kij​=1/(n−1) for i≠ji\neq ji=j. On Σ2\Sigma_2Σ2​, write p=(r,1−r)p=(r,1-r)p=(r,1−r) and q=(s,1−s)q=(s,1-s)q=(s,1−s). Since there is only one edge, the distance reduces to the scalar Riemannian length

WK(p,q)=∣∫sr duθ(u,1−u)∣,0<r,s<1.\mathcal W_K(p,q) = \left|\int_s^r \frac{\d u}{\sqrt{\theta(u,1-u)}}\right|, \qquad 0<r,s<1.WK​(p,q)=∣∣​∫sr​θ(u,1−u)​du​∣∣​,0<r,s<1.
(49)

This formula is closed but not Euclidean: the logarithmic mean changes the cost of moving mass depending on the current split between the two states.

On Σ3\Sigma_3Σ3​, the complete-neighbor graph is a triangle. For p∈int⁡(Σ3)p\in\operatorname{int}(\Sigma_3)p∈int(Σ3​), set

aij(p):=12θ(pi,pj),1≤i<j≤3.a_{ij}(p)\eqdef\frac12\theta(p_i,p_j), \qquad 1\leq i<j\leq3.aij​(p):=21​θ(pi​,pj​),1≤i<j≤3.
(50)

For a tangent vector u∈R3u\in\RR^3u∈R3 with u1+u2+u3=0u_1+u_2+u_3=0u1​+u2​+u3​=0, orient the edges as 1→21\to21→2, 1→31\to31→3, 2→32\to32→3. The squared norm induced by the discrete Wasserstein metric is

∥u∥p2=min⁡q12,q13,q23{q122a12+q132a13+q232a23},\|u\|_p^2 = \min_{q_{12},q_{13},q_{23}} \left\{ \frac{q_{12}^2}{a_{12}}+ \frac{q_{13}^2}{a_{13}}+ \frac{q_{23}^2}{a_{23}} \right\},∥u∥p2​=q12​,q13​,q23​min​{a12​q122​​+a13​q132​​+a23​q232​​},
(51)

subject to

u1+q12+q13=0,u2−q12+q23=0,u3−q13−q23=0.u_1+q_{12}+q_{13}=0, \qquad u_2-q_{12}+q_{23}=0, \qquad u_3-q_{13}-q_{23}=0.u1​+q12​+q13​=0,u2​−q12​+q23​=0,u3​−q13​−q23​=0.
(52)

Eliminating the three edge fluxes gives an explicit formula. With D=a12−1+a13−1+a23−1D=a_{12}^{-1}+a_{13}^{-1}+a_{23}^{-1}D=a12−1​+a13−1​+a23−1​,

q12∗=u2/a23−u1/a13D,q13∗=−u1−q12∗,q23∗=q12∗−u2,q_{12}^*=\frac{u_2/a_{23}-u_1/a_{13}}{D}, \qquad q_{13}^*=-u_1-q_{12}^*, \qquad q_{23}^*=q_{12}^*-u_2,q12∗​=Du2​/a23​−u1​/a13​​,q13∗​=−u1​−q12∗​,q23∗​=q12∗​−u2​,
(53)

and ∥u∥p2\|u\|_p^2∥u∥p2​ is obtained by inserting these values in (51). Therefore

WK2(p0,p1)=inf⁡pt∈int⁡(Σ3)∫01∥p˙t∥pt2  dt,p0=p0,p1=p1.\mathcal W_K^2(p^0,p^1) = \inf_{p_t\in\operatorname{int}(\Sigma_3)} \int_0^1\|\dot p_t\|_{p_t}^2\,\d t, \qquad p_0=p^0, \quad p_1=p^1.WK2​(p0,p1)=pt​∈int(Σ3​)inf​∫01​∥p˙​t​∥pt​2​dt,p0​=p0,p1​=p1.
(54)

Thus the three-state distance is an explicit two-dimensional Riemannian geodesic problem on the open triangle. The formula is simple enough to compute directly, but it already shows the main difference with Euclidean geometry on the simplex: the local metric depends nonlinearly on the current density through logarithmic edge mobilities.

show_book_figure("discrete-markov-simplex-distances")

Discrete Wasserstein distances on small Markov-chain simplices. The left panel shows the closed-form profiles p↦WK(ap,ap0)p\mapsto \mathcal W_K(a_p,a_{p_0})p↦WK​(ap​,ap0​​), with ap=(p,1−p)a_p=(p,1-p)ap​=(p,1−p), for several anchors p0p_0p0​ on Σ2\Sigma_2Σ2​. The middle panel shows numerical level sets of WK(a,aˉ)\mathcal W_K(a,\bar a)WK​(a,aˉ) on Σ3\Sigma_3Σ3​, where aˉ=(1/3,1/3,1/3)\bar a=(1/3,1/3,1/3)aˉ=(1/3,1/3,1/3), using the local Riemannian norm induced by the complete-neighbor Markov chain. The right panel shows the corresponding level sets for the ordinary W2W_2W2​ distance with d(i,j)=1d(i,j)=1d(i,j)=1 for i≠ji\neq ji=j, so that W22(a,aˉ)=∥a−aˉ∥TVW_2^2(a,\bar a)=\norm{a-\bar a}_{\mathrm{TV}}W22​(a,aˉ)=∥a−aˉ∥TV​.

References¶
  1. Dacorogna, B., & Moser, J. (1990). On a Partial Differential Equation Involving the Jacobian Determinant. Annales de l’Institut Henri Poincaré C, Analyse Non Linéaire, 7(1), 1–26.
  2. Benamou, J.-D., & Brenier, Y. (2000). A computational fluid mechanics solution to the Monge-Kantorovich mass transfer problem. Numerische Mathematik, 84(3), 375–393.
  3. Dolbeault, J., Nazaret, B., & Savaré, G. (2009). A new class of transport distances between measures. Calculus of Variations and Partial Differential Equations, 34(2), 193–231.
  4. Liero, M., Mielke, A., & Savaré, G. (2016). Optimal transport in competition with reaction: the Hellinger–Kantorovich distance and geodesic curves. SIAM Journal on Mathematical Analysis, 48(4), 2869–2911.
  5. Chizat, L., Schmitzer, B., Peyré, G., & Vialard, F.-X. (2018). An interpolating distance between optimal transport and Fisher–Rao metrics. Foundations of Computational Mathematics, 18(1), 1–44.
  6. Liero, M., Mielke, A., & Savaré, G. (2018). Optimal entropy-transport problems and a new Hellinger–Kantorovich distance between positive measures. Inventiones Mathematicae, 211(3), 969–1117.
  7. Chizat, L., Peyré, G., Schmitzer, B., & Vialard, F.-X. (2018). Unbalanced optimal transport: dynamic and Kantorovich formulation. Journal of Functional Analysis, 274(11), 3090–3123.
  8. Maas, J. (2011). Gradient flows of the entropy for finite Markov chains. Journal of Functional Analysis, 261(8), 2250–2292.
  9. Mielke, A. (2013). Geodesic convexity of the relative entropy in reversible Markov chains. Calculus of Variations and Partial Differential Equations, 48(1–2), 1–31.
  10. Chow, S.-N., Huang, W., Li, Y., & Zhou, H. (2012). Fokker-Planck equations for a free energy functional or Markov process on a graph. Archive for Rational Mechanics and Analysis, 203(3), 969–1008.
  11. Erbar, M., & Maas, J. (2014). Gradient flow structures for discrete porous medium equations. Discrete and Continuous Dynamical Systems, 34(4), 1355–1374.
  12. Erbar, M., Rumpf, M., Schmitzer, B., & Simon, S. (2017). Computation of optimal transport on discrete metric measure spaces. arXiv Preprint arXiv:1707.06859.
